name: Osteoarthritis Management Protocol
description: A comprehensive, evidence-based protocol for the management of Osteoarthritis (OA) in primary care, aligning with the American College of Rheumatology (ACR) 2019 Guidelines for the Management of Osteoarthritis.

templateData.duration: Initial phase: 12 weeks. Ongoing management: Lifelong.
goals: Reduce pain by at least 30% on a Visual Analog Scale (VAS) within 12 weeks.
Improve physical function (e.g., walking distance, ability to perform daily activities) by 20% within 12 weeks.
Increase joint range of motion (ROM) by 10 degrees in affected joint within 6 months.
Minimize disease progression and prevent joint deformity through sustained management.
Improve patient's quality of life as measured by validated questionnaires (e.g., WOMAC, KOOS).

items: Patient education on OA, self-management strategies, and prognosis.
Weight management for overweight/obese patients (target 5-10% body weight reduction).
Structured exercise program (aerobic, strengthening, balance, flexibility) tailored to affected joint(s).
Physical therapy referral for individualized exercise prescription, manual therapy, and assistive device training.
Occupational therapy referral for joint protection techniques and activity modification.
Topical NSAIDs (e.g., diclofenac gel) for localized pain.
Oral NSAIDs (e.g., naproxen, ibuprofen) for moderate to severe pain, used cautiously.
Acetaminophen for mild to moderate pain (consider limited efficacy).
Intra-articular corticosteroid injections for acute flares or moderate-to-severe pain not responsive to oral/topical agents.
Assistive devices (e.g., cane, walker) for ambulation support.
Bracing/taping for joint stability and pain relief.
Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) for chronic pain management and mood disorders.
Referral to Orthopedic Surgery for patients with severe symptoms, functional limitation, and failure of conservative management (e.g., consideration for total joint arthroplasty).
Referral to Rheumatology for atypical presentations, inflammatory features, or diagnostic uncertainty.
Referral to Pain Management for complex chronic pain not adequately controlled.

medications.name: Topical Diclofenac Gel 1%
medications.instructions: Apply to affected joint area. Wash hands thoroughly after application. Avoid contact with eyes and mucous membranes.

medications.name: Naproxen
medications.instructions: Take with food or milk to minimize gastrointestinal upset. Use lowest effective dose for shortest duration. Consider concomitant proton pump inhibitor (PPI) if GI risk factors present.

medications.name: Acetaminophen
medications.instructions: Do not exceed maximum daily dose. Be aware of acetaminophen content in combination products. Use cautiously in patients with liver impairment.

medications.name: Triamcinolone Acetonide (Intra-articular)
medications.instructions: Administered by a qualified healthcare professional into the joint space. Aseptic technique is crucial. Monitor for post-injection flare or infection.

lifestyle: Engage in regular low-impact exercise (e.g., swimming, cycling, walking) for at least 150 minutes per week.
Maintain a healthy body weight through balanced diet and regular physical activity.
Incorporate strength training exercises for muscles surrounding affected joints.
Practice joint protection techniques (e.g., using larger joints for tasks, avoiding prolonged static positions).
Ensure adequate rest and sleep to aid in pain management and recovery.
Utilize assistive devices (e.g., cane, walker) as needed for support and stability.
Apply heat or cold packs to affected joints for temporary pain relief.
Avoid activities that exacerbate joint pain or cause excessive stress on affected joints.

followUp.frequency: Every 4-6 weeks initially, then every 3-6 months once stable.
metrics: Pain intensity (e.g., VAS, Numeric Rating Scale)
Physical function (e.g., WOMAC, KOOS, 6-minute walk test)
Joint range of motion and stability
Patient satisfaction and quality of life
Presence of new symptoms or disease progression

warnings: Avoid chronic use of oral NSAIDs in patients with high cardiovascular risk, renal insufficiency, or gastrointestinal bleeding history.
Acetaminophen should be used cautiously in patients with liver disease; do not exceed 3000 mg/day.
Intra-articular corticosteroid injections should be limited to 3-4 per joint per year due to potential cartilage damage.
Opioids are generally NOT recommended for routine OA management due to lack of efficacy evidence and significant risks.
Glucosamine and chondroitin are NOT recommended due to lack of consistent efficacy.
Duloxetine should be considered only for patients with chronic widespread pain or significant contraindications to NSAIDs.
Exercise programs should be initiated gradually and tailored to individual tolerance to avoid exacerbating pain.
Patients should be educated on signs of infection post-injection (e.g., increased pain, redness, swelling, fever) and to seek immediate medical attention.
